one_line_takeaway: A taxonomy-and-evaluation survey of LLM unlearning methods that organizes the field into five method families and the TOFU/WMDP/BLUR-era benchmark landscape, concluding the field lacks formal forgetting guarantees and remains vulnerable to adversarial relearning -- while its own comparison figure prominently favors the authors' prior UNLEARN method.
key_finding: Per the survey's synthesis of prior published results (not new experiments by this paper): traditional methods (preference optimization, gradient ascent, KL minimization, gradient difference) consistently fall below the p=0.1 forget-quality significance threshold across all three forget-set sizes, i.e., they fail to produce statistically significant forgetting. FLAT achieves near-significant forget quality while maintaining strong utility. NPO achieves statistically significant forgetting at all forget-set sizes but its model utility deteriorates sharply as forget-set size grows. UNLEARN (the authors' own prior method) is reported as achieving statistically significant forgetting at all forget-set sizes while keeping normalized model utility near 1.0, with the margin over other methods widening as forget-set size increases. Separately, the survey highlights BLUR (Hu et al., 2025), a benchmark that inserts forget-related terms into retain questions to probe forget/retain overlap, under which 'existing methods often degrade sharply' and simple baselines sometimes outperform more sophisticated ones.
core_contribution: A survey (not an empirical study) that (1) proposes a taxonomy of LLM unlearning methods split into data-centric, parameter-centric, architecture-centric, hybrid, and 'other' (prompt-level and system-level) approaches; (2) reviews the unlearning evaluation ecosystem (benchmarks, metrics, datasets); and (3) synthesizes open challenges (forgetting guarantees, forget/retain boundary ambiguity, governance, efficiency, cross-language/cross-modal unlearning, and adversarial relearning). The paper's own original contribution is the taxonomy/organization and a synthesized comparison figure (Fig. 10) plotting prior methods' TOFU results together; it reports no new experiments, models, or attacks of its own. Authored by the same group (Lizzo and Heck) behind the UNLEARN subspace-projection method, which the survey cites repeatedly and highlights as the strongest performer in its own comparison.
threat_model: The paper does not define or analyze a threat model of its own -- it is a methods/evaluation survey, not an attack or defense paper. It does describe, as part of its literature review, security-relevant motivations and risks raised by cited work: (a) privacy/compliance drivers for unlearning (GDPR, CCPA 'right to be forgotten'); (b) hazardous-knowledge removal via the WMDP benchmark, which measures biosecurity, cybersecurity, and chemical-weapons knowledge (Li et al., 2024); (c) 'adversarial relearning' as an open problem -- forgotten information re-emerging via fine-tuning, prompt engineering, or adversarial extraction, citing a multimodal VLM extraction attack (Patil et al., 2025) and a 'Stealthy Unlearning Attack' that adds perturbation noise to sidestep the forgetting process (Zhang et al., 2025b). These are reported as findings from cited third-party work, not threats analyzed or evaluated by this paper's own authors.
limitations: This is a review of the paper as written, not an original empirical study, so standard experimental limitations (sample size, statistical power, ablations) do not apply. Key concerns specific to this survey: (1) Conflict of interest -- the authors are also the creators of UNLEARN, which the survey's own comparison figure (Fig. 10) presents as outperforming every other method reviewed; the survey does not flag this as a potential source of bias. (2) The TOFU comparison numbers are second-hand (pulled from each cited paper's own reported results under differing experimental setups), so cross-method comparability is not independently verified by this survey. (3) The paper explicitly acknowledges several field-wide open problems as unsolved: no formal/certifiable forgetting guarantees exist at LLM scale (differential-privacy bounds degrade for over-parameterized models); forget/retain boundaries are ill-defined and often artificially disjoint in benchmarks; cross-lingual unlearning does not transfer (forgetting in one language does not unlearn it in others, per Lu and Koehn 2025); multimodal unlearning resources remain immature; and adversarial relearning/extraction can recover 'forgotten' knowledge. A Limitations/Ethics Statement section header exists in the PDF but its body text was not captured in the extracted pages.

datasets_used: TOFU (Maini et al., 2024) -- synthetic memorized facts about imaginary authors; used for the survey's own Fig. 10 comparison across 1%/5%/10% forget-set sizes
WHP / 'Who's Harry Potter' (Eldan and Russinovich, 2023) -- fictional QA forget benchmark
WMDP (Li et al., 2024) -- biosecurity, cybersecurity, and chemical-weapons knowledge benchmark
RWKU (Jin et al., 2024) -- real-world knowledge about well-known individuals
BLUR (Hu et al., 2025) -- combined forget/retain queries testing overlap robustness
MMLU (Hendrycks et al., 2021) -- 57-subject general knowledge retention benchmark
HELM (Liang et al., 2023) -- multidimensional evaluation (accuracy, robustness, calibration, fairness)
BIG-Bench (Srivastava et al., 2023) -- broad task suite from 400+ researchers
OK-VQA / UnLOK-VQA (Patil et al., 2025) -- multimodal unlearning benchmark
SAFEERASER (Chen et al., 2025a) -- multimodal dataset of 3000 images and roughly 29.8K VQA pairs, per the extracted text
quantitative_results: Traditional methods (preference optimization, gradient ascent, KL minimization, gradient difference) fall below the forget-quality significance threshold (p<0.1) at all TOFU forget-set sizes (1%, 5%, 10%) -- per prior papers as synthesized in this survey's Fig. 10
FLAT: near-significant forget quality (just below the p=0.1 threshold) with strong retained model utility
NPO: statistically significant forget quality (p>0.1) at all forget-set sizes, but model utility deteriorates sharply as forget-set size increases

UNLEARN (authors' own prior method): statistically significant forgetting at all forget-set sizes with normalized model utility maintained near 1.0; gap over other methods widens as forget-set size grows
SISA training (Bourtoule et al., 2021) is cited as demonstrating 'significant speedups' for image classification and tabular data via sharded retraining (no LLM-scale numbers given)
baselines_compared: Gradient difference / corrective gradient-descent (Liu et al., 2022)
KL-divergence minimization on retain set (Wang et al., 2025)
FLAT -- Forget-data-only Loss AdjustmenT (Wang et al., 2024c)
Negative Preference Optimization / NPO (Zhang et al., 2024)
UNLEARN -- subspace projection with subspace discrimination (Lizzo and Heck, 2025a)
